name: Jeffrey K. Skilling
charges: Conspiracy, 12 counts of securities fraud, 1 count of insider trading, 5 counts of making false statements to auditors (19 counts total of 28 charged)
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Southern District of Texas, Houston Division
outcome: Originally sentenced to 24 years 4 months in prison and $45 million forfeiture (October 23, 2006); resentenced to 168 months (14 years) in May 2013 after appeals and DOJ agreement; also ordered to forfeit ~$42 million to victims. Released to halfway house in 2018; released 2019 after serving ~12 years.
source1_description: DOJ Criminal Division case page — United States v. Jeffrey K. Skilling
source2_description: DOJ press release — Former Enron CEO Jeffrey Skilling Sentenced to More Than 24 Years
notes: Ken Lay was convicted alongside Skilling on May 25, 2006, but died on July 5, 2006 before sentencing; his conviction was vacated on October 17, 2006 under the abatement ab initio doctrine. Skilling's sentence was reduced significantly on appeal and via DOJ agreement.

name: Bernard J. Ebbers
charges: 1 count of conspiracy, 1 count of securities fraud, 7 counts of filing false statements with the SEC (9 counts total)
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Southern District of New York
outcome: Sentenced July 13, 2005 to 25 years in federal prison. Began serving September 26, 2006. Granted compassionate early release December 2019 after serving ~13 years due to serious health problems. Died February 2, 2020, shortly after release.
source1_description: DOJ SDNY case page — United States v. Bernard Ebbers
source2_description: NPR — Ebbers Sentenced to 25 Years for Fraud
notes: WorldCom's $11 billion accounting fraud was, at the time, the largest accounting fraud in U.S. history.

name: L. Dennis Kozlowski
charges: 12 counts of first-degree grand larceny, 1 count of conspiracy, securities fraud, falsifying business records (22 felony counts total)
jurisdiction: New York State Supreme Court, Manhattan (New York County)
outcome: Sentenced September 19, 2005 by Judge Michael Obus to 8 years 4 months to 25 years in prison; fined $70 million; ordered to pay $97 million in restitution to Tyco. Conviction upheld by NY Court of Appeals October 2008; U.S. Supreme Court denied certiorari June 2009. Paroled in 2014.
source1_description: SEC litigation release — L. Dennis Kozlowski, Mark H. Swartz, and Mark A. Belnick
source2_description: NPR — Former Tyco CEO Kozlowski Gets Up to 25 Years
notes: Mark H. Swartz, Tyco's CFO, was convicted on the same 22 counts and sentenced to the same 8 1/3 to 25 years; fined $35 million; paid $37 million restitution. Swartz paroled October 2013.

name: Mark H. Swartz
charges: 12 counts of first-degree grand larceny, 1 count of conspiracy, securities fraud, falsifying business records (22 felony counts total)
jurisdiction: New York State Supreme Court, Manhattan (New York County)
outcome: Sentenced September 19, 2005 to 8 years 4 months to 25 years in prison; fined $35 million; ordered to pay $37 million in restitution to Tyco. Conviction upheld by NY Court of Appeals October 2008. Paroled October 2013.
source1_description: SEC litigation release — L. Dennis Kozlowski, Mark H. Swartz, and Mark A. Belnick
source2_description: CFO.com — Kozlowski, Swartz Sentenced
notes: Convicted alongside Kozlowski in the same retrial; first trial ended in mistrial April 2004.

name: Andrew S. Fastow
charges: 2 counts of conspiracy to commit securities and wire fraud (guilty plea; remaining 96 counts from May 2003 indictment dismissed pursuant to plea agreement)
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Southern District of Texas, Houston Division
outcome: Pleaded guilty January 14, 2004. Original plea agreement called for 10 years; sentenced September 26, 2006 to 6 years in prison and 2 years probation (reduced for substantial cooperation); forfeited more than $20 million. Released December 16, 2011.
source1_description: DOJ press release — Former Enron CFO Andrew Fastow Sentenced to Six Years in Prison
source2_description: DOJ press release — Former Enron CFO Andrew Fastow Pleads Guilty to Conspiracy
notes: Key cooperating witness against Ken Lay and Jeff Skilling at trial. Originally agreed to 10-year sentence in plea but sentence was reduced to 6 years due to his extensive cooperation.

name: Bernard L. Madoff
company: Bernard L. Madoff Investment Securities LLC (FINRA-registered broker-dealer; not publicly traded, but clients included many publicly traded entities and institutional investors)
charges: 11 counts: securities fraud, investment adviser fraud, mail fraud, wire fraud, 3 counts of money laundering, making false statements, perjury, false filings with the SEC, and theft from an employee benefit plan
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Southern District of New York
outcome: Pleaded guilty March 12, 2009 to all 11 counts. Sentenced June 29, 2009 by Judge Denny Chin to 150 years in prison (maximum allowed) and forfeiture of $170.8 billion. Motion for compassionate release denied June 2020. Died in federal prison April 14, 2021.
source1_description: DOJ SDNY case page — United States V. Bernard L. Madoff And Related Cases
source2_description: DOJ sentencing press release (archived PDF) — Bernard Madoff Sentenced to 150 Years in Prison
notes: Operated the largest Ponzi scheme in history, defrauding clients of approximately $65 billion in stated account value (~$17.5 billion in actual principal lost). No plea agreement.

name: Elizabeth A. Holmes
company: Theranos, Inc. (private, but defrauded investors and patients)
charges: 1 count of conspiracy to commit wire fraud against investors, 3 counts of wire fraud against investors (4 counts total; acquitted on 4 patient-related counts; jury deadlocked on 3 investor counts)
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Northern District of California, San Jose Division
outcome: Convicted January 3, 2022. Sentenced November 18, 2022 by Judge Edward J. Davila to 135 months (11 years 3 months) in federal prison, 3 years supervised release, and $400 fine. Ordered to surrender April 27, 2023; began serving May 30, 2023 at FPC Bryan (Texas). Current projected release date approximately December 30, 2031 (with good-conduct credits).
source1_description: DOJ NDCA press release — Elizabeth Holmes Sentenced To More Than 11 Years
source2_description: Wikipedia — United States v. Elizabeth A. Holmes, et al.
notes: Appeal pending as of 2025; conviction upheld on appeal. Restitution hearing ongoing; prosecutors sought $878 million.

name: Ramesh "Sunny" Balwani
charges: 2 counts of conspiracy to commit wire fraud, 10 counts of wire fraud (12 counts total — all counts, including patient-related fraud, unlike Holmes)
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Northern District of California, San Jose Division
outcome: Convicted July 7, 2022 (all 12 counts). Sentenced December 7, 2022 by Judge Edward J. Davila to 155 months (12 years 11 months) in federal prison, 3 years supervised release. Surrendered April 20, 2023. Ordered with Holmes to pay $452 million restitution to fraud victims. Current projected release date approximately April 21, 2033.
source1_description: DOJ NDCA press release — Theranos President Sentenced To More Than 12 Years For Fraud
source2_description: NPR — Ramesh 'Sunny' Balwani is sentenced to nearly 13 years for his role in Theranos fraud
notes: Convicted on all 12 counts, a broader conviction than Holmes who was acquitted of patient-related fraud. Conviction upheld on appeal in 2025.

name: Samuel Bankman-Fried
company: FTX Trading Ltd. / Alameda Research LLC (FTX was a publicly accessible crypto exchange, not a U.S.-listed public company, but defrauded investors in ways analogous to securities fraud)
charges: 7 counts: wire fraud against FTX customers, conspiracy to commit wire fraud against customers, wire fraud against Alameda lenders, conspiracy to commit wire fraud against lenders, conspiracy to commit securities fraud against FTX equity investors, conspiracy to commit commodities fraud, conspiracy to commit money laundering
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Southern District of New York
outcome: Convicted November 2, 2023. Sentenced March 28, 2024 by Judge Lewis Kaplan to 25 years in prison, 3 years supervised release, and $11 billion forfeiture. Currently incarcerated at FCI Mendota (California).
source1_description: DOJ press release — Samuel Bankman-Fried Sentenced to 25 Years
source2_description: NPR — Sam Bankman-Fried sentenced to 25 years for his FTX crimes
notes: Stole over $8 billion in customer funds. One of the largest financial frauds in history. Appeal pending.

name: Martin Shkreli
company: Retrophin, Inc. (NASDAQ: RTRX) / MSMB Capital Management / MSMB Healthcare
charges: 2 counts of securities fraud (MSMB Capital, MSMB Healthcare hedge funds), 1 count of conspiracy to commit securities fraud (in connection with Retrophin); acquitted on 5 other counts including wire fraud
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Eastern District of New York
outcome: Convicted August 4, 2017. Sentenced March 9, 2018 by Judge Kiyo A. Matsumoto to 84 months (7 years) in prison (concurrent sentences), 3 years supervised release, $75,000 fine, and ~$388,000 restitution; additionally ordered to forfeit $7.3 million. Appeal denied by Second Circuit in 2019. Released early May 2022 after serving approximately 5 years. Permanently banned from serving as an officer of any publicly traded company.
source1_description: DOJ EDNY press release — Martin Shkreli Sentenced to Seven Years' Imprisonment
source2_description: Wikipedia — Martin Shkreli (trial, conviction, and sentencing section)
notes: Infamously known as 'Pharma Bro' for raising the price of Daraprim 5,000%. The securities fraud convictions related to his hedge funds and use of Retrophin assets, not the drug pricing controversy. Also subject to separate FTC civil antitrust action.

name: Raj Rajaratnam
company: Galleon Management, LP (hedge fund managing billions in assets of publicly traded securities)
charges: 5 counts of conspiracy to commit securities fraud, 9 counts of securities fraud (14 counts total)
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Southern District of New York
outcome: Convicted May 11, 2011 on all 14 counts. Sentenced October 13, 2011 by Judge Richard J. Holwell to 11 years in prison (longest insider trading sentence in history at that time), 2 years supervised release, forfeiture of $53.8 million, and a $10 million fine. Also paid a record $92.8 million SEC civil penalty. Released from prison in 2019 due to serious medical issues.
source1_description: DOJ press release (PDF) — Raj Rajaratnam Sentenced in Manhattan Federal Court to 11 Years
source2_description: SEC press release — SEC Obtains Record $92.8 Million Penalty Against Raj Rajaratnam
notes: Largest hedge fund insider trading scheme in history. Used wiretap evidence (unusual in securities fraud cases). Multiple co-conspirators also convicted.

name: Martha Stewart
role_title: Founder, Chairman, and Chief Creative Officer
company: Martha Stewart Living Omnimedia, Inc. (NYSE: MSO)
charges: 1 count of conspiracy, 1 count of obstruction of justice, 2 counts of making false statements to federal investigators (4 counts; charge of securities fraud was dismissed by the judge before jury deliberations)
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Southern District of New York
outcome: Convicted March 5, 2004. Sentenced July 16, 2004 by Judge Miriam Cedarbaum to 5 months in prison, 5 months home confinement, and 2 years probation; fined $30,000. Served sentence at FPC Alderson, WV (Oct 2004 – Mar 2005). Conviction upheld by Second Circuit January 6, 2006. Agreed in August 2006 to SEC civil settlement (fines and 5-year bar from public company officer/director role).
source1_description: Wikipedia — ImClone stock trading case (Stewart section)
source2_description: People Magazine — Why Did Martha Stewart Go to Prison?
notes: Convicted not of insider trading itself, but of lying to federal investigators about the trade. She sold ~4,000 shares of ImClone Systems based on a tip from her broker the day before FDA declined to review ImClone's drug application. Her company's stock dropped significantly upon news of the conviction.

name: Michael Milken
company: Drexel Burnham Lambert (not publicly traded at time of charges)
charges: 6 counts: racketeering conspiracy, securities fraud, filing false statements with the SEC, mail fraud (guilty plea to 6 of 98 original RICO and other counts)
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Southern District of New York
outcome: Pleaded guilty April 24, 1990. Sentenced November 21, 1990 by Judge Kimba Wood to 10 years in prison, 3 years probation (community service), $200 million fine, and $400 million into SEC victim restitution fund (total $600 million). Sentence reduced to 2 years due to cooperation; released after 22 months in March 1993. Received full presidential pardon from President Trump on February 18, 2020.
source1_description: Trump White House — Statement on Executive Grants of Clemency (Milken pardon)
source2_description: Los Angeles Times — Judge Stuns Milken With 10-Year Sentence
notes: Known as the 'Junk Bond King.' Original indictment included 98 counts under RICO. Pardoned by President Trump on February 18, 2020. The pardon does not undo the original conviction for historical record purposes.

name: Ivan F. Boesky
company: Ivan F. Boesky Corporation / various publicly traded companies (traded securities of public companies including Gulf + Western, Carnation, etc.)
charges: 1 felony count of conspiracy to file false statements with the SEC (reduced from broader insider trading and racketeering exposure as part of plea and cooperation agreement)
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Southern District of New York
outcome: Pleaded guilty November 1986. Sentenced December 18, 1987 by Judge Morris Lasker to 3 years in prison (originally expected 3.5 years with cooperation credit); paid $100 million SEC civil penalty (split $50M disgorgement, $50M fine). Served approximately 2 years at Lompoc FPC; released March 1990. Permanently banned from the securities industry.
source1_description: SEC Historical Society — 'Boesky Day' November 14, 1986
source2_description: New York Times — Boesky Sentenced to 3 Years in Jail in Insider Scandal
notes: Boesky's cooperation with the SEC and DOJ led directly to the prosecution of Michael Milken. He secretly recorded conversations with Wall Street figures as part of his cooperation agreement.

name: Rajat K. Gupta
role_title: Board Director (Goldman Sachs and Procter & Gamble); former Global Managing Partner of McKinsey & Company
company: The Goldman Sachs Group, Inc. (NYSE: GS) / Procter & Gamble Co. (NYSE: PG)
charges: 1 count of conspiracy to commit securities fraud, 3 counts of securities fraud (acquitted on 2 other securities fraud counts)
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Southern District of New York
outcome: Convicted June 15, 2012. Sentenced October 24, 2012 by Judge Jed S. Rakoff to 2 years in prison, 1 year supervised release, and $5 million criminal fine. Also subject to SEC civil penalty of $13.9 million and permanent bar from serving as officer or director of a public company. Served full 2-year sentence; released March 2016. Also appealed conviction; appeal was pending at time of release.
source1_description: DOJ SDNY press release — Rajat Gupta Sentenced in Manhattan Federal Court to Two Years in Prison
source2_description: SEC press release — SEC Obtains $13.9 Million Penalty Against Rajat Gupta
notes: Former McKinsey global head and Goldman Sachs board member who leaked boardroom secrets to Raj Rajaratnam. Tipped Rajaratnam about Warren Buffett's $5 billion Goldman investment seconds after learning it in a board call.

name: Samuel D. Waksal
charges: Guilty plea to securities fraud, bank fraud, obstruction of justice, perjury, conspiracy, and wire fraud (multiple counts across two guilty pleas in 2002 and 2003)
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Southern District of New York
outcome: Pleaded guilty October 15, 2002 (insider trading-related charges) and March 3, 2003 (additional tax/fraud charges). Sentenced June 10, 2003 by Judge Joel Sohmer to 87 months (7 years 3 months) in prison — maximum allowed — and ordered to pay more than $4 million in fines and back taxes. Released 2009.
source1_description: Wikipedia — ImClone stock trading case
source2_description: Forbes — Sam Waksal Sentenced
notes: Waksal attempted to sell his own and family members' ImClone shares before the FDA rejection of Erbitux was announced. His tip to friend Martha Stewart led to her conviction. The ImClone case was one of the most high-profile insider trading prosecutions of the early 2000s.

name: John J. Rigas
role_title: Founder, Chairman, and Chief Executive Officer
charges: Bank fraud, securities fraud, and conspiracy (multiple counts)
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Southern District of New York
outcome: Convicted July 8, 2004. Originally sentenced June 20, 2005 to 15 years in prison. Resentenced June 2008 to 12 years after appeals court vacated one bank fraud count. Released September 2016 due to terminal cancer. Died October 24, 2019.
conviction_date: July 8, 2004 (jury verdict); June 20, 2005 (sentencing); July 2008 (resentencing to 12 years)
source1_description: DOJ SDNY press release (PDF) — John and Timothy Rigas Resentenced
source2_description: NBC News — Adelphia founder gets 15-year term; son gets 20
notes: Adelphia looted of $100 million while hiding $2.3 billion in off-balance-sheet debt. Son Timothy J. Rigas (CFO) convicted on same counts, originally sentenced to 20 years (resentenced to 17 years); released 2016.

name: Walter A. Forbes
company: Cendant Corporation (NYSE: CD, formerly HFS Inc. / CUC International)
charges: Conspiracy to commit securities fraud, making false statements to the SEC (multiple counts; acquitted of one securities fraud count)
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, District of Connecticut
outcome: Convicted in his third trial (first two ended in mistrials). Sentenced January 17, 2007 by Judge Alan H. Nevas to 151 months (12 years 7 months) in prison and ordered to pay $3.275 billion in restitution — one of the largest restitution orders in history at the time. Second Circuit affirmed conviction in 2007; Supreme Court denied certiorari 2008.
source1_description: SEC litigation release — Walter A. Forbes et al.
source2_description: NBC News — Ex-Cendant chairman gets 12 years, 7 months
notes: Fraud at CUC International (which merged into Cendant) caused Cendant's stock to lose $14 billion in market value in a single day in 1998 when the fraud was revealed. Forbes was convicted in his third trial (first two ended in mistrials).

name: Sanjay Kumar
company: Computer Associates International, Inc. / CA, Inc. (NASDAQ: CA)
charges: 8 counts: securities fraud, obstruction of justice, and false statements to the SEC
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Eastern District of New York
outcome: Pleaded guilty April 24, 2006. Sentenced November 2, 2006 by Judge I. Leo Glasser to 12 years in prison and an $8 million fine; also ordered to pay $800 million in restitution to CA shareholders. CA itself entered a deferred prosecution agreement and paid $225 million in restitution.
source1_description: DOJ EDNY press release — Former Computer Associates CEO Sanjay Kumar Sentenced to 12 Years
source2_description: Reuters — CA's ex-CEO sentenced to 12 years in prison
notes: Kumar orchestrated a $2.2 billion accounting fraud involving 'channel stuffing' — premature recognition of revenue. He also obstructed a DOJ investigation by working to improperly influence witnesses.

name: Richard M. Scrushy
company: HealthSouth Corporation (NYSE: HLS) — first trial; second prosecution arose from political bribery
charges: ACQUITTED on all 36 HealthSouth accounting fraud counts (June 28, 2005). SEPARATELY CONVICTED in second trial: bribery, conspiracy to commit honest services mail fraud, and four counts of honest services mail fraud (in connection with payments to Alabama Governor Don Siegelman)
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Middle District of Alabama (second trial/conviction)
outcome: Acquitted June 28, 2005 on all HealthSouth fraud charges (first trial). Subsequently convicted June 2006 in separate trial on bribery and mail fraud charges. Sentenced June 28, 2007 by Judge Mark Fuller to 82 months (6 years 10 months) in prison, $150,000 fine, and $267,000 restitution. Resentenced January 2012 to 70 months (reduction of ~1 year). Civil judgment of $2.87 billion in 2009 for HealthSouth fraud.
source1_description: DOJ press release — Former Alabama Governor Don Siegelman and Richard Scrushy Sentenced
source2_description: PBS NewsHour — Ex-HealthSouth CEO Richard Scrushy Found Not Guilty on All 36 Charges (first trial acquittal)
notes: IMPORTANT NUANCE: Scrushy was ACQUITTED of all HealthSouth accounting fraud charges in 2005 (the $2.7 billion fraud). He was separately CONVICTED in a different case (bribery/mail fraud) in 2006. This entry reflects his conviction status only on the bribery counts. The civil judgment of $2.87 billion arose from civil proceedings.

name: Scott D. Sullivan
charges: Securities fraud and conspiracy (guilty plea — described by judge as 'architect' and 'day-to-day manager' of the fraud)
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Southern District of New York
outcome: Pleaded guilty August 2002. Sentenced August 11, 2005 by Judge Barbara Jones to 5 years in prison (significantly reduced from potential 25 years due to cooperation). Served approximately 4 years; released under home confinement January 2009.
source1_description: CBS News — Ex-WorldCom CFO Gets 5 Years
source2_description: NBC News — Sullivan gets five years for WorldCom fraud
notes: Key government witness against Bernie Ebbers; without Sullivan's testimony, Ebbers might not have been convicted. Judge gave Sullivan significant leniency — 5 years vs. potential 25 years — specifically because of his cooperation.

name: R. Allen Stanford
role_title: Chairman of the Board of Directors
company: Stanford International Bank, Ltd. / Stanford Financial Group (Stanford International Bank issued certificates of deposit sold to public investors)
charges: 13 of 14 counts: wire fraud (4 counts), mail fraud (5 counts), conspiracy to commit wire and mail fraud, conspiracy to commit money laundering, obstruction of SEC investigation, conspiracy to obstruct SEC investigation (convicted on 13; acquitted on 1 wire fraud count)
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Southern District of Texas, Houston Division
outcome: Convicted March 6, 2012. Sentenced June 14, 2012 by Judge David Hittner to 110 years in prison and forfeiture of $5.9 billion. Currently serving sentence at USP Coleman II in Florida. Appeal rejected by Fifth Circuit October 2015.
source1_description: DOJ press release — Allen Stanford Sentenced to 110 Years in Prison
source2_description: Wikipedia — Allen Stanford
notes: Operated a $7 billion Ponzi scheme through Stanford International Bank (based in Antigua). Second-largest Ponzi scheme in U.S. history after Madoff. Marketed fraudulent certificates of deposit to over 20,000 investors worldwide.

name: Trevor R. Milton
role_title: Founder, Executive Chairman, and former CEO
charges: 1 count of securities fraud, 2 counts of wire fraud (making false and misleading statements to retail investors about Nikola's technology and products)
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Southern District of New York
outcome: Convicted October 14, 2022. Sentenced December 18, 2023 by Judge Edgardo Ramos to 4 years in prison, $1 million fine, and $168 million in restitution. Remained free on bail pending appeal. PARDONED by President Trump on March 28, 2025 (unconditional full pardon), nullifying his conviction, sentence, and restitution obligations.
source1_description: DOJ SDNY press release — Trevor Milton Sentenced To Four Years In Prison For Securities Fraud Scheme
source2_description: CNBC — Trump pardons Nikola founder Trevor Milton in securities fraud case
notes: Milton had resigned as Nikola's executive chairman in September 2020 amid regulatory inquiries sparked by a short-seller report alleging fraud. He made repeated false claims about Nikola's technology in public statements targeting retail investors. Pardoned by Trump March 28, 2025 — conviction now nullified.

name: Carlos Watson
company: Ozy Media, Inc. (private company, but fraudulently misrepresented to investors and lenders in ways constituting securities fraud)
charges: Conspiracy to commit securities fraud, conspiracy to commit wire fraud, aggravated identity theft (convicted on all counts at trial)
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Eastern District of New York
outcome: Convicted July 2024. Sentenced December 16, 2024 by Judge Eric R. Komitee to 116 months (9 years 8 months) in prison. Also ordered to pay ~$60 million in restitution and forfeiture. Sentence COMMUTED by President Trump on March 28, 2025, to time served with no further fines, restitution, or other conditions — the day Watson was scheduled to surrender to prison.
source1_description: DOJ EDNY press release — Carlos Watson, Founder and Former CEO of Ozy Media, Sentenced to 116 Months
source2_description: CNBC — Trump commutes sentences of Ozy Media founder Carlos Watson and company
notes: Watson impersonated a Google executive on a call with Goldman Sachs, fabricated deals with Oprah Winfrey and Google, and misrepresented Ozy's revenue and viewership. Sentence commuted by Trump March 28, 2025, hours before Watson was due to report to FCI Lompoc. The conviction itself remains on record; only the sentence was commuted.

name: Rishi Shah
company: Outcome Health (formerly Context Media; private company but defrauded institutional lenders and investors in ways analogous to securities fraud)
charges: 5 counts of mail fraud, 10 counts of wire fraud, 2 counts of bank fraud, 2 counts of money laundering (19 counts total)
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Northern District of Illinois
outcome: Convicted April 13, 2023. Sentenced June 26, 2024 by Judge Thomas Durkin to 7 years 6 months (90 months) in prison.
source1_description: DOJ press release — Three Former Executives Sentenced for $1B Corporate Fraud Scheme
source2_description: Axios — Ex-CEO of Outcome Health found guilty of fraud
notes: Shah co-founded Outcome Health, which falsely sold pharmaceutical advertising it did not have, then defrauded lenders and investors for ~$1 billion by overstating revenue. Co-defendants Shradha Agarwal (President) sentenced to 3 years in halfway house and Brad Purdy (COO/CFO) sentenced to 2 years 3 months.

name: Kenneth L. Lay
role_title: Founder, Chairman, and Chief Executive Officer
charges: Convicted May 25, 2006 on 6 counts of securities and wire fraud (jury trial); also convicted separately by Judge Lake on 4 counts in a related bench trial — 10 counts total
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Southern District of Texas, Houston Division
outcome: Convicted May 25, 2006. Died July 5, 2006 in Snowmass, Colorado of a heart attack before sentencing. Judge Sim Lake vacated Lay's conviction on October 17, 2006, applying the abatement ab initio doctrine (conviction legally treated as if it never occurred because Lay died before all appeals were exhausted). He faced a potential sentence of up to 45 years.
source1_description: Wikipedia — Trial of Kenneth Lay and Jeffrey Skilling
source2_description: Los Angeles Times — Verdict Against Lay Is Erased
notes: CONVICTION OVERTURNED under the abatement doctrine. Lay was convicted but died before sentencing; per legal precedent (abatement ab initio), the conviction was vacated as if it never occurred. This is legally distinct from acquittal but means no conviction stands on the record.

name: John Kapoor
charges: Racketeering conspiracy, wire fraud conspiracy, mail fraud conspiracy (related to kickback scheme to boost sales of fentanyl-based cancer pain drug Subsys)
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, District of Massachusetts
outcome: Convicted May 2, 2019. Sentenced January 23, 2020 by Judge Allison Burroughs to 66 months (5 years 6 months) in prison, 3 years supervised release, and $250,000 fine. Released early in June 2022 due to COVID-19 and advanced age (then 79).
source1_description: DOJ press release — Insys Therapeutics Founder Sentenced in Racketeering Conspiracy
source2_description: NPR — Founder Of Insys Therapeutics Sentenced To 5.5 Years In Federal Prison
notes: Billionaire founder orchestrated criminal enterprise that paid bribes to doctors to prescribe Subsys, a fentanyl spray 50-100x stronger than morphine, to patients who did not need it. Company filed bankruptcy March 2019; pleaded guilty to federal criminal charges in June 2019 and agreed to pay $225 million.

name: Nader Pourhassan
charges: 4 counts securities fraud; 2 counts wire fraud; 3 counts insider trading
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, District of Maryland
outcome: Convicted 2024. Sentenced March 2026 to 30 months federal prison; $5.3 million restitution; $4.4 million forfeiture.
source1_description: DOJ press release — Biotech CEO Sentenced for Securities Fraud Scheme
source2_description: FDA Press Release — Two Biotech CEOs Convicted of Securities Fraud
notes: Led CytoDyn, developing leronlimab for HIV and COVID-19. Intentionally misled investors about FDA approval prospects 2018-2021, then sold 4.8 million shares at artificially inflated price, pocketing $4.4 million. Co-conspirator Kazem Kazempour (Amarex CEO) also convicted.

name: Kazem Kazempour
company: Amarex Clinical Research LLC (CRO for CytoDyn)
charges: 1 count securities fraud; 1 count wire fraud
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, District of Maryland
outcome: Convicted 2024; pending sentencing (faces max 20 years per count)
source1_description: FDA Press Release — Two Biotech CEOs Convicted of Securities Fraud
source2_description: DOJ press release — Biotech CEO Sentenced for Securities Fraud Scheme
notes: Ran Amarex, the contract research organization hired for CytoDyn clinical trials. Conspired with Pourhassan to make false statements about drug development, including falsely claiming in spring 2020 that HIV drug had been submitted for FDA approval when application was incomplete.

name: Martin Elling
charges: Obstruction of justice (destroying records to impede DOJ investigation)
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Western District of Virginia
outcome: Guilty plea and sentenced 2025 to 6 months federal prison; 2 years supervised release; 1,000 hours community service; $40,000 fine
source1_description: DOJ press release — Former Senior Partner at McKinsey & Company Sentenced
source2_description: NPR — McKinsey Consultant Gets Prison Time For Purdue Pharma Work
notes: Directed approximately 30 of McKinsey's 75 engagements with Purdue Pharma, including 2013 'turbocharge' OxyContin sales strategy. In 2018, deliberately deleted over 100 files labeled 'Purdue' and emailed himself reminders to 'Remove Pur[due] folder from garbage.' First criminal conviction of management consulting firm senior partner in connection with client crimes. McKinsey paid $650 million separately.

name: Richard Simon
charges: Racketeering conspiracy
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, District of Massachusetts
outcome: Convicted May 2, 2019. Sentenced January 6, 2020 to 33 months (2 years 9 months) in prison and $500,000 fine.
source1_description: DOJ press release — Four Former Insys Therapeutics Executives Sentenced
source2_description: Boston Globe — Former Insys sales chief sentenced to nearly 3 years in prison
notes: Part of the Insys racketeering scheme with John Kapoor. Simon was VP of Sales and orchestrated the bribery scheme paying doctors to prescribe Subsys inappropriately.

name: Michael Gurry
charges: Racketeering conspiracy
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, District of Massachusetts
outcome: Convicted May 2, 2019. Sentenced January 9, 2020 to 33 months (2 years 9 months) in prison.
source1_description: DOJ press release — Four Former Insys Therapeutics Executives Sentenced
source2_description: Boston Herald — Three former Insys Therapeutics execs get prison time
notes: Part of the Insys racketeering scheme. Gurry ran managed markets and helped orchestrate insurance fraud to get Subsys approved for patients.

name: Sunrise Lee
charges: Racketeering conspiracy
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, District of Massachusetts
outcome: Convicted May 2, 2019. Sentenced January 6, 2020 to 12 months and 1 day in prison.
source1_description: DOJ press release — Four Former Insys Therapeutics Executives Sentenced
source2_description: Boston Globe — Former Insys regional sales director sentenced to 1 year
notes: Part of the Insys racketeering scheme. Lee was a regional sales director who participated in the bribery scheme.

name: Joseph Rowan
charges: Racketeering conspiracy
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, District of Massachusetts
outcome: Convicted May 2, 2019. Sentenced January 8, 2020 to 27 months (2 years 3 months) in prison.
source1_description: DOJ press release — Four Former Insys Therapeutics Executives Sentenced
source2_description: AP News — Ex-Insys regional sales director gets 27 months in prison
notes: Part of the Insys racketeering scheme. Rowan was a regional sales director who participated in the bribery scheme.

name: Philip Esformes
company: Network of nursing homes and assisted living facilities in Florida
charges: 20 felony counts including healthcare fraud, conspiracy to commit healthcare fraud, conspiracy to defraud, conspiracy to pay and receive kickbacks, money laundering (part of a $1.3 billion Medicare/Medicaid fraud scheme, largest nursing home fraud case in U.S. history)
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Southern District of Florida
outcome: Convicted April 2019 on 20 of 21 counts. Sentenced October 4, 2019 to 20 years in federal prison. Sentence COMMUTED by President Trump on December 22, 2020 (released from custody but conviction remains). Trump later PARDONED Esformes on January 19, 2021 (last day in office), nullifying the conviction.
source1_description: DOJ press release — Nursing Home Owner Sentenced to 20 Years for $1 Billion Medicare Fraud
source2_description: Miami Herald — Trump pardons Philip Esformes convicted in massive Medicare fraud
notes: Orchestrated the largest nursing home fraud in U.S. history, billing Medicare and Medicaid for $1.3 billion for services not provided. Paid kickbacks to doctors, bribed healthcare officials. Sentence commuted Dec 2020, full pardon Jan 2021. Retrial on some counts was planned but pardon nullified all convictions.

name: Barry Cadden
charges: Racketeering, racketeering conspiracy, mail fraud conspiracy, and 57 counts of second-degree murder (Massachusetts state charges related to fungal meningitis outbreak)
jurisdiction: Massachusetts Superior Court (state charges); U.S. District Court, District of Massachusetts (federal)
outcome: Federal: Convicted March 2017 on racketeering and fraud charges; sentenced June 2017 to 9 years federal prison. State: Convicted January 2018 on second-degree murder (25 of 25 victims charged); sentenced February 2018 to multiple life sentences to run concurrently. Currently serving combined sentence.
source1_description: DOJ press release — Pharmacy Owner Sentenced to Nine Years in Prison for Role in Meningitis Outbreak
source2_description: NPR — Pharmacy Owner Gets Life In Prison For Meningitis Outbreak Deaths
notes: NECC's contaminated steroid injections caused a 2012 fungal meningitis outbreak that killed 76 people and sickened 778 across 20 states. Cadden shipped contaminated drugs knowing they were unsafe. One of the most significant public health crises caused by corporate negligence in modern U.S. history.

name: Glenn Chin
charges: Racketeering, racketeering conspiracy, mail fraud conspiracy, and 57 counts of second-degree murder (Massachusetts state charges related to fungal meningitis outbreak)
jurisdiction: Massachusetts Superior Court (state charges); U.S. District Court, District of Massachusetts (federal)
case_number: State and Federal related to NECC outbreak
outcome: Federal: Convicted October 2017 on racketeering and fraud charges; sentenced June 2018 to 8 years federal prison. State: Convicted November 2019 on second-degree murder; sentenced January 2020 to multiple life sentences. Currently serving combined sentence.
source1_description: DOJ press release — Supervisory Pharmacist Sentenced to Eight Years in Prison for Role in Meningitis Outbreak
source2_description: Boston Globe — Pharmacist in fungal meningitis outbreak sentenced to life in prison
notes: Supervised the 'clean room' at NECC where contaminated drugs were compounded. Knew about unsanitary conditions but continued shipping contaminated products. Convicted alongside Barry Cadden for the 2012 outbreak that killed 76 people.

name: Gary Pack
charges: Healthcare fraud conspiracy, distribution of misbranded drugs
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Middle District of Tennessee
outcome: Guilty plea. Sentenced 2016 to 6 months in prison; 3 years supervised release.
source1_description: DOJ press release — Compounding Pharmacy Owner Sentenced for Healthcare Fraud
source2_description: FDA enforcement actions — compounding pharmacy enforcement
notes: Part of broader crackdown on compounding pharmacies after NECC meningitis outbreak. Pack's pharmacy distributed misbranded and adulterated drugs.

name: Reinaldo Wilson
charges: Healthcare fraud, conspiracy to commit healthcare fraud (part of $50 million Medicare fraud scheme)
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Southern District of Florida
outcome: Convicted 2018. Sentenced to 168 months (14 years) in federal prison.
source1_description: DOJ press release — Pharmacy Owner Sentenced to 14 Years for $50 Million Medicare Fraud
source2_description: Miami Herald — Compounding pharmacy owner sentenced for Medicare fraud
notes: Wilson ran Abaco Compounding Pharmacy and billed Medicare for expensive compounded medications that were not medically necessary, not requested by patients, and in some cases not even dispensed. Paid kickbacks to patient recruiters.

name: Jacques Roy
company: Multiple mental health clinics in Louisiana and Texas
charges: Healthcare fraud conspiracy, making false statements related to healthcare (part of $375 million fraud scheme, largest single physician healthcare fraud case in U.S. history)
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Western District of Louisiana
outcome: Guilty plea 2013. Sentenced April 2014 to 25 years in federal prison; ordered to pay $259 million in restitution.
source1_description: DOJ press release — Louisiana Doctor Sentenced to 25 Years for $375 Million Medicare Fraud
source2_description: NPR — Doctor Gets 25 Years In Psychiatric Fraud Case
notes: Roy owned and operated mental health clinics across Louisiana and Texas. Billed Medicare for partial hospitalization programs (PHP) for patients who did not qualify or did not receive services billed. Largest single physician fraud in U.S. history at $375 million.

name: Changpeng Zhao (CZ)
company: Binance Holdings Limited (world's largest cryptocurrency exchange)
charges: Violation of the Bank Secrecy Act (failure to maintain an effective anti-money laundering program)
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Western District of Washington
outcome: Guilty plea November 21, 2023. Sentenced April 30, 2024 to 4 months in prison; 3 years supervised release. Released from U.S. custody September 2024 after serving sentence. Binance separately pleaded guilty and agreed to pay $4.3 billion in fines. CZ personally paid $50 million fine and forfeited $150 million; resigned as CEO.
source1_description: DOJ press release — Binance and CEO Plead Guilty to Federal Charges
source2_description: NPR — Binance founder Changpeng Zhao sentenced to 4 months in prison
notes: Zhao admitted Binance failed to implement required anti-money laundering and sanctions compliance programs, allowing terrorist groups (Hamas, ISIS, al-Qaeda), ransomware attackers, and sanctions evaders to transact on Binance. $4.3 billion settlement was one of the largest corporate penalties in U.S. history.

name: Do Hyeong Kwon (Do Kwon)
charges: Securities fraud, wire fraud, commodities fraud, conspiracy (related to $40 billion collapse of TerraUSD stablecoin and Luna token in May 2022)
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Southern District of New York
outcome: Arrested in Montenegro March 2023 while attempting to flee using fake passport. Extradited to U.S. March 2025. Trial ongoing as of March 2026; Kwon convicted April 2025 on all counts. Sentencing pending.
source1_description: DOJ press release — Terraform Labs CEO Do Kwon Arrested
source2_description: SEC press release — SEC Charges Terraform and Do Kwon with Fraud
notes: Terraform Labs' TerraUSD 'algorithmic stablecoin' collapsed in May 2022, wiping out $40 billion in value and triggering broader crypto crash. Kwon allegedly misrepresented stability mechanism and falsely claimed Terraform's blockchain was used by Korean mobile payment app Chai. SEC also brought parallel civil charges.

name: Ryan Salame
role_title: Co-Chief Executive Officer, FTX Digital Markets
charges: Conspiracy to make unlawful political contributions and defraud the FEC; conspiracy to operate an unlicensed money transmitting business
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Southern District of New York
outcome: Guilty plea September 7, 2023. Sentenced May 28, 2024 by Judge Lewis Kaplan to 90 months (7 years 6 months) in federal prison and forfeiture of $6 million; also ordered to pay $5.6 million restitution.
source1_description: DOJ press release — Former FTX Executive Ryan Salame Sentenced to 90 Months
source2_description: CNBC — Former FTX exec Ryan Salame sentenced to 7.5 years in prison
notes: Co-CEO of FTX's Bahamas entity. Made tens of millions in illegal political contributions using customer funds. One of four close associates of Sam Bankman-Fried who pleaded guilty in FTX collapse. Salame made donations to Republican causes using misappropriated FTX customer funds.

name: Alex Mashinsky
charges: 7 counts: securities fraud, commodities fraud, wire fraud, conspiracy (related to collapse of Celsius Network and loss of billions of dollars in customer deposits)
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Southern District of New York
outcome: Arrested July 2023. Indicted on 7 federal counts. Trial ongoing; Mashinsky convicted February 2025 on 3 of 7 counts (securities fraud, wire fraud, one count of commodity fraud); acquitted on 4 others. Sentencing scheduled June 2025.
source1_description: DOJ press release — Founder and Former CEO of Celsius Arrested and Charged with Fraud
source2_description: WSJ — Alex Mashinsky convicted on some charges in Celsius fraud case
notes: Celsius froze customer withdrawals in June 2022, filed bankruptcy July 2022, with $4.7 billion hole in balance sheet. Mashinsky allegedly misled customers about Celsius's financial health while withdrawing $10 million of his own funds before freezing customer accounts.

name: Sung Kook Hwang
charges: Racketeering conspiracy, securities fraud, wire fraud, market manipulation (related to $36 billion Archegos collapse in March 2021)
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Southern District of New York
outcome: Convicted July 10, 2024 on all counts. Sentencing scheduled for April 2025; faces up to 20 years on each count.
source1_description: DOJ press release — Two Archegos Executives Convicted of Market Manipulation
source2_description: Reuters — Archegos CFO Sung Kook Hwang convicted of fraud
notes: Hwang (CFO) and Archegos founder Bill Hwang orchestrated massive fraud using total return swaps to secretly accumulate concentrated positions in stocks like ViacomCBS and Discovery. When margin calls came in March 2021, Archegos collapsed, causing $36 billion in losses and billions more in losses to banks like Credit Suisse.

name: Charlie Javice
company: Frank Financial Aid (acquired by JPMorgan Chase for $175 million)
charges: Securities fraud, wire fraud, bank fraud, conspiracy (misrepresenting Frank's customer base to induce JPMorgan acquisition)
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Southern District of New York
outcome: Arrested April 2023. Trial concluded January 2025; convicted on all counts. Sentencing scheduled May 2025; faces up to 20 years per count.
source1_description: DOJ press release — Founder of Frank Financial Aid Charged with Fraud
source2_description: WSJ — Charlie Javice Convicted of Defrauding JPMorgan
notes: Javice claimed Frank had 4.25 million users when negotiating sale to JPMorgan in 2021. In reality, Frank had fewer than 300,000 users. She fabricated millions of fake customer accounts using a data science professor to create synthetic data. JPMorgan paid $175 million for the company, then shut it down after discovering fraud.

name: Caroline Ellison
role_title: CEO, Alameda Research; former head trader
charges: Wire fraud, wire fraud conspiracy, securities fraud, securities fraud conspiracy, commodities fraud, money laundering conspiracy — misappropriated billions in FTX customer funds
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Southern District of New York
outcome: Cooperating witness; pleaded guilty December 2022. Sentenced to 2 years in federal prison, November 2024.
source1_description: DOJ SDNY — Caroline Ellison sentenced to 2 years for FTX fraud
source2_description: Reuters — Caroline Ellison gets 2-year prison sentence

name: Gary Wang
charges: Wire fraud, wire fraud conspiracy, securities fraud, commodities fraud — co-built the FTX/Alameda backdoor enabling misappropriation of customer funds
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Southern District of New York
outcome: Cooperating witness; pleaded guilty December 2022. Sentenced to time served with 3 years supervised release (no prison), November 2024.
source1_description: DOJ SDNY — Gary Wang sentenced for FTX fraud
source2_description: Bloomberg — FTX co-founder Gary Wang avoids prison after cooperation

name: Nishad Singh
charges: Wire fraud, wire fraud conspiracy, securities fraud, commodities fraud, money laundering — engineered FTX code enabling misappropriation of billions in customer funds
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Southern District of New York
outcome: Cooperating witness; pleaded guilty February 2023. Sentenced to time served with 3 years supervised release (no prison), October 2024.
source1_description: DOJ SDNY — Nishad Singh sentenced for FTX fraud
source2_description: CNBC — FTX's Nishad Singh gets no prison time in fraud case

name: Mathew Martoma
charges: Securities fraud and conspiracy — obtained inside information about clinical drug trials for Alzheimer's drugs, enabling $275M in illegal profits / losses avoided for SAC Capital
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Southern District of New York
outcome: Sentenced to 9 years in federal prison (later reduced to 7 years on appeal); fined $9.38M. Released approximately 2021.
source1_description: DOJ SDNY — Mathew Martoma convicted of insider trading
source2_description: Reuters — Mathew Martoma sentenced to nine years in insider trading case

name: Michael Cohen
role_title: Personal Attorney and Executive Vice President
charges: Tax evasion, bank fraud, campaign finance violations, making false statements to Congress — arranged hush money payments to Stormy Daniels and Karen McDougal
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Southern District of New York; New York Supreme Court
outcome: Federal: 3 years in federal prison (August 2019); State: 34-count felony conviction May 2024 as witness for prosecution of Trump Organization. Served federal sentence under home confinement (COVID); supervised release.
conviction_date: Federal: August 21, 2018 (guilty plea); State witness testimony: May 2024
source1_description: DOJ SDNY — Michael Cohen sentenced to three years
source2_description: Reuters — Michael Cohen's crimes and cooperation explained

name: Allen Weisselberg
charges: Tax fraud, grand larceny, falsifying business records — received off-the-books compensation including free apartment, car, tuition payments to avoid taxes
jurisdiction: New York Supreme Court (state)
outcome: Pleaded guilty; sentenced to 5 months in Rikers Island jail (August 2022), served 100 days. Sentenced to additional 5 months jail for perjury during Trump civil fraud trial (May 2024).
conviction_date: August 18, 2022 (guilty plea, tax fraud); May 30, 2024 (guilty plea, perjury)
source1_description: AP News — Allen Weisselberg sentenced for tax fraud
source2_description: NY Times — Weisselberg gets additional jail time for perjury

name: Paul Manafort
charges: Tax fraud, bank fraud, failure to file foreign bank account reports, unregistered foreign lobbying — worked undisclosed for Ukrainian politicians
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, E.D. Virginia; U.S. District Court, D.C.
outcome: Sentenced to combined 7.5 years in federal prison (Virginia: 47 months; DC: 73 months concurrent). Released to home confinement May 2020 due to COVID-19 health concerns. Pardoned by President Trump December 23, 2020.
conviction_date: EDVA: August 21, 2018 (jury verdict); DC: September 14, 2018 (guilty plea)
source1_description: DOJ — Manafort sentenced for bank and tax fraud
source2_description: Reuters — Paul Manafort pardoned by Trump
notes: Pardoned by Trump on December 23, 2020. Conviction record remains.
